content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

January 17, 1977

77-01-126

Mr. M. Robert Adkison
Superintendent
Ceres Unified School District
Post Office Box 307
Sixth and Lawrence Streets
Ceres, California 95307

Dear Mr. Adkison:

You seek advice with respect to your District's requirement
to disclose certain interests pursuant to a Conflict of Interest
Code adopted under Government Code §87302 et seq. Your District
is wholly within one county and thus, its code reviewing body is
the Stanislaus County Board of Supervisors.

First, you ask whether agriculturally zoned real property
within your District may be excluded from real property disclosure
pursuant to your Code. In general, the staff of the Commission
has perceived no reason why agriculturally zoned real property
should be the subject of disclosure pursuant to a school district
Code. Real property seems to be a reasonable subject of disclo-
sure within a Conflict of Interest Code for a school district
because of the potential purchase of real property for new school
sites, and/or the changing of boundaries within a residential
district which may increase or decrease the value of such resi-
dential property. The foreseeability of such financial effects
occurring with respect to real property which is zoned agricul-
turally seems more remote than is the case for residential
property. Therefore, if a school district and its board of
supervisors come to the same or similar conclusion, it would
be permissible pursuant to the Political Reform Act to purge
such agriculturally zoned real property from the disclosure
categories for the District. These determinations are, of
course, a local matter. The applicable standard is found in
Government Code §87302(b), which provides that the interests
which must be disclosed are those which may foreseeably be
materially financially affected by the decision-making capacity
of the governmental official.

You also ask whether the disclosure requirements for
investments in business entities which contract with the
District may be limited by the following phrase:

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (21A 377-6443) __________FILENAME (PCS)

M. Robert Anderson
January 17, 1977                                                    Page Two


     ... excluding those regulated by the Federal
Securities and Exchange Commission, ...


     In our view, such exclusion is too broad. In general, it is
our view that certain school districts and small districts
cannot materially affect, by virtue of their purchasing
capacity, giant corporations. Thus, some methodology may
be permissible to exclude such giant corporations from the
list of business entities subject to disclosure by school
district personnel. However, the Federal Securities and
Exchange Commission regulates an enormous number of corpor-
ations, both large and small; thus, an exclusion based upon
an entity's regulation by the Securities and Exchange Commis-
sion is too broad. As for an applicable excluding phrase,
perhaps the following would suffice:

     ... excluding those listed on the New York
Stock Exchange, ...

     Stocks listed on the New York Stock Exchange seem
sufficiently large to preclude District decisions from
having a foreseeable material financial effect upon them.

     You ask about a similar exclusion for income from sources
which are regulated by the SEC. Inclusion of such language in
your income disclosure category is unnecessary, since income
from such sources is already excluded by virtue of Government
Code §8203(b)(8). Therefore, I would strike the redundant
clause in your income disclosure category.

     Some of your disclosure categories are conditioned by a
concluding paragraph in your Exhibit B disclosure categories.
The paragraph is as follows:

     Investments and income otherwise reportable
under paragraphs (a) and (b) above shall not
be reported unless the total amount of all
contracts by the business entity to provide
services, supplies, materials, machinery, or
equipment to the Ceres Unified School District
unless the total amount of all foreseeable
contracts by the business entity to provide
services, supplies, materials, machinery,
or equipment to the Ceres Unified School
District will be more than $1,000 in the
next calendar year.

M. Robert Adkison
January 17, 1977                                                    Page Three


     The end sought by this paragraph is to eliminate the
disclosure of sources of income or business entities which
contract for only a small amount of purchases from the Dis-
trict during the year. The goal is an admirable one.
However, I have grave doubts about the arbitrary assignment
of $1,000 as the minimum below which the materiality of a
decision upon an entity would be discounted. The Commission
has rejected such arbitrary dollar amount assignments for
determinations of materiality in the past.

     In a recent conversation, you also asked about the time
at which a board member must resign in order to avoid having
to file a disclosure statement. The member must resign prior
to the effective date of the Code. The Act does not specify
when a Code shall become effective. Conflict of Interest Codes
for state agencies are effective 30 days after their filing
with the Secretary of State, which may be as much as 30 days
after their approval by the Fair Political Practices Commission.
The Commission has established by regulation that Conflict of
Interest Codes of multi-county districts be final 30 days
after their approval by the Commission. Local code reviewing
bodies have similar reasonable discretion to set effective
dates sometime after the approval of a Code by the board of
supervisors.

     I hope that the foregoing has proved of some assistance
to you. If you have further questions, please call me at
916/322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:gjb

sections.question_synthetic: The Superintendent of Ceres Unified School District seeks advice on whether agriculturally zoned real property should be excluded from conflict of interest disclosure, whether SEC-regulated investments can be excluded from disclosure, what constitutes an appropriate dollar threshold for materiality in business entity disclosures, and when board members must resign to avoid filing disclosure statements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Agriculturally zoned real property may be excluded from disclosure if the school district and county supervisors agree, given remote financial effects; SEC-based exclusions are too broad but New York Stock Exchange listing exclusions would be permissible; income from SEC-regulated sources is already excluded by statute; arbitrary $1,000 materiality thresholds are problematic and the Commission has rejected such assignments; board members must resign prior to the Code's effective date, which local reviewing bodies may set reasonably after approval.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains multiple distinct advisory questions and responses. Chief Spurlock addresses four separate inquiries point-by-point. Extraction treats entire body as implicit Q&A structure. High confidence due to clear intent and thorough analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The Superintendent of Ceres Unified School District seeks advice on whether agriculturally zoned real property should be excluded from conflict of interest disclosure, whether SEC-regulated investments can be excluded from disclosure, what constitutes an appropriate dollar threshold for materiality in business entity disclosures, and when board members must resign to avoid filing disclosure statements.

CONCLUSION: Agriculturally zoned real property may be excluded from disclosure if the school district and county supervisors agree, given remote financial effects; SEC-based exclusions are too broad but New York Stock Exchange listing exclusions would be permissible; income from SEC-regulated sources is already excluded by statute; arbitrary $1,000 materiality thresholds are problematic and the Commission has rejected such assignments; board members must resign prior to the Code's effective date, which local reviewing bodies may set reasonably after approval.

embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Spurlock provides guidance to the Ceres Unified School District on multiple conflict of interest code disclosure issues, including real property zoning exclusions, securities regulation-based investment exclusions, and materiality thresholds for business entity disclosures.
